ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific term for packaged less-sugar fruit tea RTD; 'tea beverage' (FOODON_03301099) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FJP covers tea-based beverages broadly; no specific code exists for packaged less-sugar fruit tea RTD in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for packaged less-sugar fruit tea RTD beverage.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 250 ml (one bottle), providing about 80 kcal and 16.75 g of sugar from this less-sugar packaged fruit tea.

nutrient_source.title: Fruit Tea (Less Sugar) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Fruit Tea (Less Sugar, Packaged), CR ID D09020036. All per-100g values are as reported.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for packaged less-sugar fruit tea RTD in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 55 by analogy with sweetened fruit-flavoured tea drinks and dilute sugar-containing beverages reported in Atkinson et al. (2008) and the University of Sydney GI Database. GI 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low category (<=55) per standard GI classification. Glycemic load per 250 ml serving calculated as: GL = (GI x carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (55 x 19.95) / 100 = 11.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Added sugar content (6.7 g/100 ml) exceeds the 5 g/100 ml beverage threshold; habitual sugar-sweetened beverage consumption independently raises blood pressure risk (AHA 2018).
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid this beverage despite its low sodium (5 mg/100 ml), as the 6.7 g/100 ml sugar content exceeds the 5 g/100 ml SSB gate and regular SSB intake is independently associated with elevated blood pressure; choose plain unsweetened brewed tea instead.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 6.7 g sugar/100 ml and zero dietary fibre; liquid sugar raises postprandial blood glucose rapidly and 'less sugar' labelling does not confer suitability for T2DM management.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this packaged fruit tea as it is a sugar-sweetened beverage (6.7 g sugar/100 ml, 0 g fibre) that causes rapid postprandial glucose elevation; plain unsweetened brewed tea or water with fresh fruit slices are preferred alternatives per ADA dietary guidelines.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages is associated with increased triglycerides, weight gain, and cardiovascular risk; this product contains 6.7 g sugar/100 ml with no protective nutrients (zero fibre, zero omega-3, zero potassium reported).
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit or avoid this packaged less-sugar fruit tea, as habitual SSB intake raises triglycerides and cardiovascular risk; the absence of fibre, potassium, and any cardioprotective nutrient means it provides no cardiovascular benefit, and unsweetened tea is a strongly preferred alternative.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar (6.7 g/100 ml) with zero fibre and no anti-inflammatory nutrients promotes pro-inflammatory mediators; no positive anti-inflammatory factor is present.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this sweetened packaged beverage as its added sugar content promotes inflammatory mediators and it contains no anti-inflammatory factors (no omega-3, polyphenols, fibre >3 g, or anti-inflammatory spices); plain brewed fruit tea without added sugar is preferred.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fruit Tea, Less Sugar (Packaged, Ready-to-Drink) is a commercially produced, ultra-processed beverage consisting of water, tea extract, fruit flavouring, and a reduced quantity of added sugar. It is sold in sealed bottles or cartons and requires no preparation. The flavour profile is mildly sweet and fruity with a light tea base. Despite the 'less sugar' designation, this product contains 6.7 g of sugar per 100 ml and 7.98 g of total carbohydrate per 100 ml, delivering approximately 32 kcal per 100 ml. A standard 250 ml serving provides roughly 80 kcal and 16.75 g of sugar. The beverage contains negligible protein (0 g/100 ml) and fat (0 g/100 ml) by product formulation, and zero dietary fibre. Sodium is very low at 5 mg/100 ml. Clinically, this product is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) under ADA, AHA, and HPB Singapore guidelines regardless of the 'less sugar' label, because added sugar is present and fibre is absent. It is unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease due to rapid glucose absorption, SSB-associated blood pressure elevation, and pro-inflammatory effects of added sugar. The glycemic index is estimated at 55 (low, upper boundary) by analogy with similar dilute sweetened tea beverages. It is widely consumed in Singapore as a convenient packaged drink at breakfast or as a snack. Known in Chinese as 低糖果茶, in Malay as teh buah kurang gula, and in Tamil as குறைந்த சர்க்கரை பழ தேநீர். Healthier alternatives include plain unsweetened brewed fruit tea or water infused with fresh fruit slices.

Is less sugar fruit tea suitable for hypertension?

Fruit tea less sugar glycemic index

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID D09020036) with confidence 0.95 as reported in the input record. Source is an approved primary regional source for Singapore food records. GI is an analogous estimate (medium confidence for GI component only) documented with approved source reference.
notes: protein_g is confirmed zero by product formulation — this is a water-based sweetened tea beverage containing no protein ingredients. fat_g is confirmed zero by product formulation — no fat-containing ingredients are present in this beverage. saturated_fat_g is confirmed zero by product formulation. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero; no fibre-containing ingredients are present. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid data for this packaged less-sugar fruit tea are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry D09020036 as of 2026-04-16. calcium_mg and iron_mg are reported as 0.0 in the HPB source. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database data for sweetened fruit-flavoured tea beverages; GI 55 is classified as low (upper boundary <=55) per standard GI classification; no direct GI measurement for this specific product was found. Multilingual local names added for Chinese (低糖果茶（瓶装即饮）/ 低糖果茶（瓶裝即飲）), Malay (Teh buah kurang gula), Tamil (குறைந்த சர்க்கரை பழ தேநீர்), and Hindi (कम चीनी फ्रूट टी) as this product is sold and consumed in Singapore's multilingual market context. gi_category corrected from 'medium' to 'low' as GI 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low category (<=55) per University of Sydney GI classification standards.

q1_food_identity.notes: Canonical English food name is precise and unambiguous. Local-language names now present for Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi, satisfying G6 multilingual requirements for a product sold in Singapore's multilingual market.

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: energy_kcal (32.0), protein_g (0.0), fat_g (0.0), carbohydrate_g (7.98) all present and numeric. Zero values for protein and fat are justified in notes as confirmed zero by product formulation. Source is HPB Singapore Foodo (approved primary source), exact match CR ID D09020036.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g (0.0), sodium_mg (5.0), saturated_fat_g (0.0), sugar_g (6.7) all present as numeric values, plausible and supported by HPB source.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index (55), gi_category corrected to 'low' (GI 55 is at the upper boundary of the low category <=55 per University of Sydney classification), glycemic_load_per_serving (11.0), gi_source all present. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008 (approved GI source), estimation basis documented.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn FOODON_03301099 (tea beverage) and FoodEx2 A0FJP (tea-based beverages) are defensible closest_parent matches. SNOMED CT null is documented with gap note. No ontology codes are invented.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description is well over 200 characters, explains what the food is, how it is prepared, taste/texture, clinical relevance, key nutrients, cultural context, and multilingual names. common_user_queries and meal_context are populated including multilingual queries.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions present: Hypertension (moderate), Type 2 Diabetes (high), Cardiovascular Disease (moderate). Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance sentence. human_condition_name for type2_diabetes is 'Type 2 Diabetes' as required. Additional condition rheumatoid_arthritis preserved from input.

Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' as GI 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low category (<=55) per University of Sydney GI classification, resolving Q4 failure.

veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: Record passes all 6 hard gates and scores 8/8. Three critical fixes applied from iteration 2 validator feedback: (1) G6 multilingual naming compliance resolved by adding Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names; (2) Q1 food identity resolved by same multilingual additions; (3) Q4 gi_category corrected from 'medium' to 'low' as GI 55 is at the upper boundary of the low classification. Q8 aliases consistency verified — all aliases_by_language strings confirmed present verbatim in flat aliases array.
